model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The cosine schedule substantially hurt early learning under the 256-step budget, so revert it and bias for faster initial optimization instead. A higher Adam learning rate with no weight decay should improve short-horizon fit on this small CNN better than the current conservative setup.","secondary_modes":["caching","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current setup is fitting quickly but still likely overconfident within a short 256-step horizon. Mild label smoothing is a very low-complexity regularization change that often improves CIFAR-10 validation loss without slowing training or destabilizing the now-good optimizer settings.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer change helped, so the next likely bottleneck is model capacity rather than training dynamics. A modest width increase in both the conv trunk and MLP head should improve representational power within the same short budget, while removing the slightly harmful label smoothing keeps the comparison clean.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent gains suggest capacity is still the main lever. Adding one more pooled conv block is a simple way to increase feature hierarchy and channel growth while also shrinking the classifier input spatially, which can improve CIFAR-10 loss without introducing optimizer complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The extra depth clearly overran the short training horizon, so revert to the stronger 2-block model and instead make Adam more responsive. Lowering beta2 should let second-moment estimates adapt faster in just 256 steps, which is often beneficial for short-budget CNN training.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The model is still operating in a very short-horizon regime, and the more responsive Adam betas improved loss materially. A moderate LR increase is the next simplest probe to see whether the current configuration is still under-stepping and can convert the fixed 256 updates into faster loss reduction.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The higher learning rate overshot, so revert to the stronger 1e-3 setting and use the fixed-step budget to process more examples per update. With only 256 optimizer steps, a larger batch is a simple way to increase data coverage and often improves short-horizon validation loss on CIFAR-10.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strong gain from moving to batch size 128 suggests the fixed 256-step regime is still bottlenecked by data coverage more than by update count. Pushing batch size further is the simplest follow-up to test whether seeing substantially more unique training examples within the same budget continues to reduce validation loss.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last two improvements came overwhelmingly from increasing data throughput under the fixed 256-step budget, indicating the run is still data-limited rather than optimization-limited. Moving to batch size 512 is the cleanest continuation of that trend and should let the model cover multiple effective passes through the dataset within the same verifier horizon.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The earlier scheduler failure happened in a much more undertrained regime. At batch size 512 the run now spans multiple effective passes through the data within 256 steps, so decaying the learning rate over the actual verifier horizon is much more likely to help refine the later updates instead of just slowing initial learning.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The scheduler again hurt this workload, so revert to the stronger constant-LR setup and use the now-proven high-data-throughput regime to test a modest capacity increase. A small width bump is the cleanest next step because the model is already seeing enough data per verifier run that added representation power may convert directly into lower validation loss.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The width increase did not help, so return to the stronger 16-channel model and probe a cleaner regularized optimizer for the now much longer effective training regime. With batch size 512 and multiple passes through the data, AdamW plus mild weight decay is a reasonable next test for improving generalization without reintroducing scheduler complexity.","secondary_modes":["layout","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"AdamW with mild weight decay improved over the prior best, which suggests the current longer effective training regime benefits from some regularization. Increasing weight decay modestly is the simplest local follow-up to test whether a bit more shrinkage further improves validation loss without changing the successful optimizer or batch configuration.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The stronger weight decay overshot slightly, which suggests the current regime may benefit more from gentler optimization than from additional regularization. With AdamW at batch size 512 and multiple effective epochs, a modest learning-rate reduction while returning to the better 1e-4 decay is a clean test of whether later updates need to be less aggressive.","secondary_modes":["caching","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The lower learning rate regressed, so restore the stronger 1e-3 setting and test whether AdamW at batch size 512 prefers a steadier second-moment estimate. The earlier beta2 reduction helped in a smaller-batch, shorter-effective-horizon regime; with much larger batches and multiple effective epochs, beta2=0.999 may generalize better.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The beta2 rollback did not help, while the strongest gains in this run have consistently come from increasing effective data throughput. Restoring the more responsive beta setting and pushing batch size beyond 512 is the highest-upside remaining probe for this fixed-step regime, where seeing more data per update has repeatedly dominated finer optimizer tweaks.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The move past batch size 512 regressed, so return to the strongest throughput setting and probe a cheaper form of extra capacity. Increasing only the classifier hidden width keeps the successful convolutional trunk unchanged while testing whether a slightly richer head can capitalize on the stronger AdamW plus high-batch regime.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The larger head was essentially neutral, so return to the stronger compact classifier and test a very mild dropout level instead. With batch size 512 and AdamW now giving multiple effective passes through the data, a small amount of activation noise may improve validation loss more cleanly than heavier explicit regularization did.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The added dropout was clearly harmful, but the strongest improvements in this run came from increasing batch size before the jump to 768 overshot. A final interpolation at batch size 640 keeps the best AdamW setup intact while testing whether the optimum data-throughput point lies between 512 and 768.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}
